metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `relates_to` reciprocal-link TOCTOU in the buffered pass: since the forward LINK event is only in the buffer (not committed) when `_is_active_link` checks the reverse direction, the plan must specify how the reciprocal idempotency check is kept correct — e.g., by consulting the pending buffer in addition to disk, or by flushing before the reciprocal write, or by noting that the importer never imports bidirectional relates_to pairs and documenting the invariant.
findings.finding: The `relates_to` reciprocal-link idempotency check is a TOCTOU in the buffered path: `add_dependency` calls `_is_active_link(target_id, source_id, ...)` to decide whether to write the reciprocal LINK event, but in buffered mode the forward LINK (just composed) is only in the in-memory buffer — not yet committed to disk — so `_is_active_link` always returns False and the reciprocal is unconditionally written. In per-event mode the forward LINK is already committed before the reciprocal check runs (line 264 of graph/_links.py). A `relates_to` import into a store that already has the forward link from a previous import will produce a duplicate reciprocal LINK event.
findings.location: graph/_links.py lines 262–267 (add_dependency reciprocal-link write) + plan section 'Scope' / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: In the buffered path, before calling the reciprocal `_is_active_link` check, also scan the pending buffer for a matching (target_id, source_id, relates_to) entry; if found, skip writing the reciprocal. Alternatively, document as a known invariant that the importer skips reciprocal relates_to writes (since both directions will be in `created_records` and both will be written in their own pass-2b iteration), and suppress the reciprocal `_write_link_event` call in `add_dependency` when called with a buffer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the 'Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism)' section how `add_dependency`'s two-write `relates_to` sequence (primary + reciprocal `_write_link_event`) is handled under the buffer opt-in — e.g., whether `add_dependency` receives the buffer directly and passes it to both `_write_link_event` calls, and how the reciprocal `_is_active_link` guard is evaluated correctly when the primary write is not yet committed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that Pass 2b buffers events through `add_dependency` → `_write_link_event` → buffered `append_event`, but `add_dependency` actually issues TWO `_write_link_event` calls for `relates_to` links (lines 262–267 of `_links.py`), and the second call's guard (`_is_active_link(target_id, source_id, ...)`) reads COMMITTED disk state to decide whether to write the reciprocal. Under the buffer opt-in, the first write is not committed before the guard runs — so the guard will always see the reciprocal as absent and unconditionally buffer a second (spurious) reciprocal LINK event. The plan's 'Deferred-commit opt-in' section never addresses this within-function two-write sequence, leaving the processing protocol for `relates_to` buffered writes fully unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the `buffer` kwarg through `add_dependency` → `_write_link_event` for both writes, and change the reciprocal guard to also check whether `(target_id, source_id, 'relates_to')` is already in the buffer (not just on committed disk), so the guard correctly skips the duplicate in buffered mode. Spec this contract explicitly in the Deferred-commit opt-in section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the byte-identical replay claim in AC3 — e.g. after running both the batched and per-event import against the same fixture, assert `git -C <tracker> diff <per_event_ref> <batched_ref> -- tickets/` produces no output (or compare exported JSON snapshots with `diff <(rebar export --ref A) <(rebar export --ref B)`).
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'the import replays to state byte-identical to the per-event path' but provides no proving command — no diff tool, no hash comparison, no structured assertion — making this claim unverifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how passes 2a–2d remain idempotent on partial-failure re-run (e.g. state-read guards in the write commands already prevent re-applying a set parent or duplicate active link, and each such guard is enumerated; or a per-pass 'already-done' marker is written before the flush so re-runs skip completed passes).
findings.finding: Passes 2a–2d (parents, links, file-impact/verify-commands, comments) have no stated idempotency mechanism for partial-failure re-runs: if the import crashes after some batch chunks of a pass are committed but before the pass completes, a re-run re-issues ALL operations for ALL `created_records`, which can produce duplicate EDIT, LINK, FILE_IMPACT, VERIFY_COMMANDS, and COMMENT events.
findings.location: Scope section / AC bullet 4 ('Idempotent re-import … skips already-imported events') / import_ndjson.py Passes 2a–2d
findings.suggested_fix: For each pass 2a–2d, explicitly enumerate the in-command guard that makes it idempotent: e.g. `edit_ticket` with an already-set parent is a no-op (verify this), `_is_active_link` skips existing links, `set_file_impact`/`set_verify_commands` are idempotent by overwrite, `comment` is NOT naturally idempotent. For comments specifically, either (a) track committed source_id+comment_index pairs in a small sidecar file flushed atomically with each batch, or (b) document that comment deduplication relies on source provenance fields already preventing re-import of the same logical comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC1's 'every non-importer caller is unchanged' claim — either broaden the test command to `pytest tests/ -q` (full suite) or add a focused regression run such as `pytest tests/ -k 'not import' -q` that exercises non-importer write paths with `buffer` defaulting to `None`.
findings.finding: AC1 claims 'the default committing path is unchanged for every non-importer caller', but the only mandated test command is `pytest tests/ -k import -q`, which filters to import tests only and will not execute non-importer call paths. The claim is therefore unverifiable by the stated proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; AC5 (`make lint && make typecheck && python -m pytest tests/ -k import -q`)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5's test clause with `python -m pytest tests/ -q` (drop the `-k import` filter) so the full regression suite guards the unchanged-default-path claim, or add a second pytest invocation: `python -m pytest tests/ -k 'not import' -q` as an explicit non-importer regression step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring a synthetic import that INCLUDES status transitions and asserts the resulting commit delta equals `sum_over_passes_1_2a_2d(ceil(pass_events/256)) + status_transition_count` (one commit per status event), confirming Pass 2e was not accidentally buffered.
findings.finding: Pass 2e's 'remains per-event' invariant has no AC that requires a test with status transitions to verify it was not accidentally wired through the buffer. AC3 deliberately excludes status transitions, so if Pass 2e were mistakenly buffered, all ACs could still pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 and the 'Pass 2e (statuses) remains per-event' requirement in Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'A synthetic N-event NDJSON file that includes M status transitions produces a commit delta of `sum_over_independent_passes(ceil(pass_events/256)) + M`, confirming Pass 2e remains per-event; additionally, `python -m pytest tests/ -k status_cascade` passes with correct cascade ordering on the imported tickets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` to the plan's named edit targets and specify that `create_core` and `edit_core` must accept and forward the `buffer` kwarg to `append_event` for Passes 1 and 2a.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_commands/composer.py is not listed as an edit target, but Pass 1 (CREATE) routes through rebar.create_ticket → composer.create_core → append_event, and Pass 2a (parent edit) routes through rebar.edit_ticket → composer.edit_core → append_event. To thread the buffer kwarg from the importer down to append_event for these passes, composer.create_core and composer.edit_core must also accept and forward the buffer parameter — yet composer.py appears nowhere in the plan's Scope or file-edit list.
findings.location: Scope section / 'What' section (Pass 1 and Pass 2a descriptions)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'src/rebar/_commands/composer.py: create_core and edit_core accept an optional buffer: list[tuple[str,dict]] | None = None kwarg and forward it to append_event, enabling the importer to capture CREATE (Pass 1) and EDIT (Pass 2a) events into its per-pass buffer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section how the `buffer` threading handles `add_dependency`'s dual `_write_link_event` calls for `relates_to` links, specifically whether both the forward and reciprocal writes land in the importer's buffer and how the reciprocal `_is_active_link` guard behaves when the forward event is not yet committed.
findings.finding: The plan threads the buffer kwarg at the _write_link_event / _seam.append_event layer for Pass 2b, but add_dependency calls _write_link_event TWICE for 'relates_to' links (once for the forward link at line 262, once for the reciprocal at line 267). The plan does not address whether both writes land in the same buffer, and the reciprocal-write guard (_is_active_link read at line 264) reads committed state — state that won't be committed yet if the forward LINK is still in the buffer.
findings.location: Scope section / 'What' section (Pass 2b description) / src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 262-267

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full call-chain threading path for the `buffer=` argument: name explicitly whether `rebar.create_ticket`, `rebar.link` (and any intermediaries such as `create_core`, `link_core`, `add_dependency`, `_write_link_event`) receive new `buffer=` kwargs, or whether an alternative threading mechanism (e.g. a context variable set by the importer) is used — and confirm the public facade signatures are intentionally changed or intentionally NOT changed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding `buffer=` to `_seam.append_event` and 'the leaf/graph write helpers that front it', but the public API call chain for Pass 1 (importer calls `rebar.create_ticket` → `_lib_writes.create_ticket` → `create_core` → `append_event`) and Pass 2b (importer calls `rebar.link` → `link_core` → `add_dependency` → `_write_link_event` → `append_event`) involves multiple public and semi-public API layers. The plan does not specify how the `buffer` argument threads through the `rebar.create_ticket` / `rebar.link` public facade functions without changing their published signatures, nor how the importer gains access to pass the buffer through these facades.
findings.location: Plan section 'Scope', AC1, AC2; src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py lines 191, 235 (calls to create_ticket and link)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC3 (or in a new 'Verification methodology' subsection) what artifact is compared to assert byte-identical state (e.g., the sorted contents of each ticket's event-log files, or the working-tree JSON snapshot) and sketch how the test produces both the batch and per-event outputs for comparison.
findings.finding: AC3 requires the test assert that a batch import 'replays to state byte-identical to the per-event path', but the plan body never describes what 'byte-identical' means in this context (commit SHAs would differ; does it mean ticket JSON on disk? event file contents? working-tree snapshot?) or how the comparison would be implemented. This leaves the AC unverifiable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: '…the import replays to state byte-identical to the per-event path'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to AC3 such as: 'Byte-identical is defined as: for every ticket created during the import, the concatenated, newline-delimited event-log file contents (sorted by event timestamp) are identical between a batch run and a reference per-event run on the same input fixture.' This pins the comparison artifact without requiring commit-SHA equality.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in the plan and/or importer code spec) the failure semantics for a mid-pass flush error: state what the importer should do on a chunk-flush exception (e.g., abort with a clear error message, and note that re-running is safe due to source_id idempotency), so operators have an explicit recovery story.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling or recovery story for a mid-pass batch flush failure (e.g., `batch_stage_and_commit` raises on chunk 5 of 12 within a single pass). While AC4 covers idempotent re-import for the full-run case, the plan does not state what state the tracker is left in after a partial-pass flush failure, nor does it specify whether the importer should retry, abort, or report a clear error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'If `batch_stage_and_commit` raises during a pass flush, the importer logs the error with the chunk index and total expected chunks, then re-raises to abort the run cleanly. The operator can safely re-run the full import; already-committed chunks are skipped via source_id idempotency (AC4).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the compose/commit split pattern, its opt-in `buffer` contract, guard-ordering invariant, and the rationale for 256-event chunks being hardcoded.
findings.finding: The compose/commit split (opt-in `buffer` kwarg on `_seam.append_event` and its frontends) is a novel architectural pattern with non-obvious invariants (guards run before append in BOTH modes; callers must not commit mid-buffer; buffer ownership is importer-side). No ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is planned, leaving future contributors without a record of why the pattern exists and what invariants must be preserved.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md (or a lightweight `docs/adr/B2-compose-commit-split.md`) that records: (1) the `buffer` kwarg contract; (2) the invariant that all state-reading guards run before the buffer append in both modes; (3) why Pass 2e is excluded; (4) why 256 is hardcoded rather than configurable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the importer assumes single-writer exclusivity for the tracker during a batch import (and document/enforce it), OR add a commit-time re-validation step (or optimistic lock / pre-commit hook check) so that guard decisions made at composition time are still valid at the deferred batch-commit moment.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a TOCTOU window between guard execution and deferred batch commit: guards read git-repo state at composition time, events sit in the buffer, and the batch flush may commit them much later — potentially after another writer (e.g., a concurrent import or manual edit) has mutated the state the guards validated (e.g., a link that was cycle-free at guard time could become cyclic before the batch commit lands).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note that the importer holds an exclusive advisory lock (e.g., a `.lock` file on the tracker) for the duration of a batch import run, so no other writer can invalidate guard-validated state between composition and flush. Alternatively, specify that the batch commit path re-checks idempotency guards just before writing, matching the existing per-event behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `buffer` opt-in on `_seam.append_event` can be avoided by having the importer's pass loops directly build `(ticket_id, event_dict)` tuples via a thin compose-only helper (bypassing `write_and_push` at the loop level) rather than threading a `buffer` kwarg through the existing seam and all its downstream helpers; document the constraint (e.g. lock non-re-entrancy, guard ordering) that makes the seam-level kwarg the only viable option if that is truly the case.
findings.finding: The `buffer: list[tuple[str, dict]] | None = None` opt-in on `_seam.append_event` (and the leaf/graph helpers it propagates to) is consumed by exactly one caller — the NDJSON importer — failing the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new abstraction.
findings.location: ## Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism) section; AC line 1 ('Compose/commit split at `_seam.append_event`')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the plan (or Scope section) why the `buffer` opt-in kwarg on `_seam.append_event` was chosen over a standalone compose-only helper (as `txn.py`'s bypass-`append_event` precedent suggests), given that only one caller (the importer) uses the non-None path.
findings.finding: The plan chooses a novel `buffer` opt-in kwarg threaded through `_seam.append_event` and every leaf/graph helper that fronts it, but does not acknowledge or justify this over the established alternative: a standalone compose-only variant (returning `(ticket_id, event_dict)` without committing) used exclusively by the importer, leaving `append_event`'s signature untouched. `txn.py` already demonstrates the 'bypass `append_event` for a non-standard commit strategy' pattern, making a compose-only variant a well-precedented approach. The chosen approach has only one real consumer but mutates the signature of a seam with ~10 call sites.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Design rationale' note to the Scope section: 'A compose-only variant (returning the event dict without committing, per the `txn.py` precedent) was considered but rejected because [reason — e.g., it would require duplicating the HLC/UUID/env_id/author envelope assembly currently inside `append_event`, while the `buffer` kwarg re-uses that assembly unchanged and guarantees identical envelopes]. The `buffer=None` default keeps all existing callers unchanged with zero risk.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC4: e.g. record `before=$(git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD)`, re-run the same import, assert `git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD` equals `before` (zero new commits), and verify event count is unchanged via a structured ticket-file assertion.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Idempotent re-import still skips already-imported events — no duplicate events, no extra commits') has no associated proving command anywhere in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Proved by: record commit count after first import; re-run `import_tickets` with the same NDJSON; assert `git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD` is unchanged and no duplicate `source_id` entries appear in any ticket JSON.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specification for minimal observability on the batch flush path: at minimum, the importer should log (at INFO level) the pass name, event count, chunk count, and elapsed time for each per-pass flush, so operators can verify correct behavior and diagnose failures.
findings.finding: No observability (logging, metrics, or progress reporting) is specified for the new batch-flush path. Operators and CI have no visibility into how many chunks were flushed per pass, whether any flush was slow/failed, or whether the commit-count reduction actually occurred as expected.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
